name: Innu
language: Innu-aimun (Montagnais-Naskapi) — closely related to Cree dialects
traditionalTerritories: Nitassinan — the ancestral homeland of the Innu, covering much of the Labrador-Quebec peninsula, including the north shore of the St. Lawrence River (Côte-Nord), Lake Saint John region, and extending north into Labrador. One of the largest traditional territories in eastern Canada. The Innu have been among the most vocal advocates for recognition of their unceded territory in Labrador (site of the Voisey's Bay nickel mine and Churchill Falls hydro development).
culturalNotes: The Innu (meaning 'the people') are the traditional peoples of the vast boreal interior of the Quebec-Labrador peninsula. They were semi-nomadic hunters following the Labrador herd of woodland caribou (atiku) across vast distances. The distinction between 'Montagnais' (southern Innu, associated with forest) and 'Naskapi' (northern Innu, associated with the Labrador tundra) is increasingly replaced by the unified term 'Innu.' The Innu culture is deeply oriented around the caribou hunt — spiritual, economic, and social life was organized around caribou. Innu oral tradition includes the Shaking Tent ceremony (matshishkapeu) and the trickster figure Mani-Utenam.
heritageSkillRelevance: Caribou hide tanning and preparation — the Innu developed sophisticated techniques for tanning caribou hides for clothing and shelter
Snowshoe construction — Innu snowshoes are designed for the deep powdery snows of the Quebec-Labrador interior
Birchbark canoe construction — Innu canoes are suited to the rivers and lakes of the Labrador interior
Smoked and dried caribou preparation (including pemmican-like preparations)
Conical tent (tipi/mishtapan) construction using caribou hides
Spruce gum waterproofing for canoes and containers
Traditional plant knowledge of the boreal forest
Ice fishing and river fishing for trout, char, and pike
communityAuthorizedResources: Musée Amérindien de Mashteuiatsh — museeamashteuiatsh.ca — Mashteuiatsh, Quebec (Pekuakamiulnuatsh-operated)
Conseil des Innus de Ekuanitshit and other Innu communities on Côte-Nord
culturalSovereigntyNotes: Caribou hide preparation, canoe building, and snowshoe construction are Level 1 with Innu attribution. Shaking Tent ceremony and other spiritual practices are Level 4 — hard block. Traditional plant medicine protocols are Level 3.
